1:
"In those days the multitude being very great, and having nothing to eat, Jesus called his disciples unto him, and saith unto them, I have compassion on the multitude." The witnesses treat the second feeding as a distinct miracle and dwell on the compassion that prompts it. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the occasion: the people had been with Christ three days, and "divers of them came from far" (a detail, Robertson notes, "alone in Mark"), so that to send them away fasting would be to let them "faint by the way." Theophylact observes that Christ "did not always work miracles concerning feeding, lest they should follow Him for the sake of food," but now intervenes because "the multitude was in danger."

Theophylact also marks the disciples' renewed unbelief: "from whence can a man satisfy these men with bread here in the wilderness?" — they "did not yet understand, nor believe in His virtue, notwithstanding former miracles." Yet "the Lord does not blame them, teaching us that we should not be grievously angry with ignorant men, but bear with their ignorance." Bede draws the practical lesson from the gathered fragments: "we should be content with what is sufficient, and not look for anything beyond."

The Greek fathers read the numbers allegorically. Theophylact takes the three days as "the baptized, for baptism is called illumination"; the seven loaves as "spiritual discourses, for seven is the number which points out the Holy Ghost, who perfects all things"; and the four thousand as "men perfect in the four virtues" — more advanced than the five thousand of the first miracle, whom he reads as "men enslaved to the five senses," and who therefore "left more fragments." So the witnesses make the verse the opening of a miracle of mercy and a test of faith: Christ's pity for the hungry crowd anticipates their need before they ask, while the disciples' forgetfulness of the first feeding sets the stage for the rebuke to come. The lesson the expositors press, both in the plain sense and the figurative, is that Christ "sends no one away fasting," but wills "all to be nourished by His grace," providing for the body that he may draw men to the bread of life.
2:
"I have compassion on the multitude, because they have now been with me three days, and have nothing to eat." The witnesses dwell on the tender compassion of Christ as the spring of the miracle. The Reformed Gill draws out its breadth: "Christ is a compassionate Saviour both of the bodies and souls of men: he had compassion on the souls of this multitude, and therefore had been teaching them sound doctrine; and he had compassion on the bodies of many of them, and had healed them of their diseases; and his bowels yearned towards them all." Barnes states it simply: "I pity their condition; I am disposed to relieve them," cross-referencing the Lord who "giveth food to the hungry" (Psalm 145:15).

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the curious construction "now three days" — "a parenthetic nominative of time" — and Clarke observes the people's real plight: "if they had brought any provisions, they were now entirely expended, and they stood in immediate need of a supply." The three days with Christ, in which the crowd had forgotten their own hunger for the sake of his teaching, are the measure both of their devotion and of his concern.

So the witnesses make the verse a window into the heart of the Saviour. The miracle that follows is not prompted by the people's request — they make none — but by Christ's own compassion, which observes their need before they feel it and provides before they ask. The expositors press the double object of that compassion, which Gill names: it reaches the soul, in the sound doctrine he had been teaching three days, and the body, in the bread he is about to multiply. The same Lord who fed their souls with the word will not send them away to faint for want of bread; and the lesson the witnesses draw is the tenderness of a Saviour whose pity embraces the whole man, anticipating both the spiritual and the bodily wants of those who follow him, and meeting them out of his own unprompted love. So the witnesses make the verse a window into the heart of the Saviour, whose unprompted pity, reaching both soul and body, observes the crowd's need before they feel it and provides before they ask, anticipating the spiritual and bodily wants of all who follow him.
3:
"And if I send them away fasting to their own houses, they will faint by the way: for divers of them came from far." The witnesses note the care of Christ for the bodily safety of the crowd, and a detail peculiar to Mark. The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the rendering — "and some of them are come from afar," the words "given as spoken by our Lord, and in the perfect tense" — and Robertson observes that "this item alone in Mark." Clarke draws out the danger: those who came from far "could not possibly reach their respective homes without perishing, unless they got food."

Gill notes the present distress: "it seems from hence that they were now fasting, and at least had had no food all that day, whatever they might have the day before." The crowd's hunger was real and immediate, and the journey home, for many, long.

So the witnesses make the verse a further proof of the Saviour's considerate compassion. Christ does not merely pity the crowd's present hunger but foresees the consequence of sending them away unfed — that some, having come from far and now spent, would "faint by the way." His care reaches beyond the moment to the road home; he provides not only against their hunger but against the danger their hunger would bring. The detail Mark alone records, that "divers of them came from far," the expositors note, both heightens the people's devotion — they had travelled a great distance and stayed three days to be with him — and deepens the picture of Christ's solicitude, which weighs each one's need and will not dismiss the weary to a journey beyond their strength. The lesson the witnesses press is the thoroughness of the Lord's compassion, which does not stop at the felt want but provides against the unfelt peril, caring for his people's whole way and not their present hour alone. The detail Mark alone records, that divers came from far, both heightens the people's devotion and deepens the picture of Christ's solicitude, which weighs each one's strength and will not dismiss the weary to a journey beyond it — caring for his people's whole way, and not their present hour alone.
4:
"And his disciples answered him, From whence can a man satisfy these men with bread here in the wilderness?" The witnesses fasten on the disciples' renewed perplexity, so soon after the first feeding. The grammatical-historical Robertson states it pointedly: "the disciples feel as helpless as when the five thousand were fed; they do not rise to faith in the unlimited power of Jesus after all that they have seen." Gill marks the forgetfulness: they were "forgetting the late miracle of feeding five thousand with five loaves and two fishes, when they had now a less number, and more provisions." Ellicott notes the abstract word for "wilderness" suggests "loneliness," a desolate place where help seemed impossible.

The disciples' question, the expositors agree, is the foil against which the miracle and the later rebuke (vv.17-21) are set. Having seen Christ feed a greater multitude with less, they ought to have looked to him at once; instead they reason only from the visible means, and find none "here in the wilderness."

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the slowness of faith. The disciples are not faithless — they have followed Christ, owned him, seen his works — yet at the very point where memory should have prompted trust, they reason as men who had never witnessed a miracle. The lesson the expositors draw, here and across this passage, is the persistence of unbelief even in those nearest Christ: how readily the heart forgets past mercies and sinks again into the helplessness of mere calculation, asking "from whence?" of a Lord whose power it has already seen overflow. The question that betrays their dulness is also, the witnesses note, the occasion of fresh grace; for Christ, instead of upbraiding them now, will answer their unbelief with a second miracle, that the lesson they failed to learn from the first might be pressed upon them again, until they should come at last to trust the unlimited power of the One who was with them in the wilderness. The question that betrays their dulness is also the occasion of fresh grace, for Christ, instead of upbraiding them, answers their unbelief with a second miracle, pressing again the lesson they failed to learn from the first, until they should trust the unlimited power of the One who was with them in the wilderness.
5:
"And he asked them, How many loaves have ye? And they said, Seven." The witnesses comment briefly, the verse being the plain prelude to the miracle, parallel to the first feeding. Gill, who carries the comment, refers to the corresponding scene, noting Christ's method of beginning from the slender means at hand. The question "how many loaves have ye?" the expositors observe across this passage, is the same Christ used before the feeding of the five thousand: he starts not from nothing but from the little the disciples possess, that the miracle may be seen to multiply what they bring.

The seven loaves, with the few small fishes named in the next verse, are the whole visible store for the great multitude; and the disciples' answer, given without expectation, sets the measure of the miracle. The expositors note the pattern, drawn out by the fathers on the first verse: that Christ takes the small provision into his hands and makes it sufficient for thousands, so that the means, however slender, become in his hands abundant.

So the witnesses make even this brief verse a quiet lesson in the method of grace. Christ asks what the disciples have, not because he needs it, but because he will work through it; he begins from the seven loaves, the little they can offer, and from that little brings forth enough for four thousand with seven baskets to spare. The lesson the expositors press is that the Lord does not despise small beginnings nor require great means, but takes what is brought — the seven loaves, the few fishes — and multiplies it by his blessing; so that the disciple who has but little to give is yet to bring it to Christ, who can make the scant store of human provision overflow for the feeding of the multitude, and the slender gift, surrendered into his hands, sufficient for a work beyond all its natural measure. The lesson the expositors press is that the Lord does not despise small beginnings nor require great means, but takes what is brought and multiplies it by his blessing, so that the slender gift, surrendered into his hands, is made sufficient for a work far beyond its natural measure.
6:
"And he commanded the people to sit down on the ground: and he took the seven loaves, and gave thanks, and brake, and gave to his disciples to set before them." The witnesses note the order of the miracle and the giving of thanks. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that the Greek for "sit down" implies "the usual Eastern position of reclining," and Robertson draws out the tenses: "brake and gave" (constative aorist followed by imperfect) — "the giving kept on," a "continuous process," the bread multiplying in the breaking as the disciples carried it to the people.

The expositors mark that Christ "gave thanks" before breaking, the same act of blessing that hallowed the first feeding; and that he distributes through the disciples, "gave to his disciples to set before them." The miracle, the witnesses note, is wrought in Christ's hands but ministered by the disciples' — a pattern of how the bread of grace is given: blessed and multiplied by Christ, distributed by his servants to the waiting multitude.

Cyril of Jerusalem, in the catechetical tradition, draws the eucharistic resonance, citing the parallel act at the Supper, where Christ "took bread, and when he had given thanks he brake it, and gave to his disciples." The breaking of bread with thanksgiving, the giving to the disciples to set before the people, recalls the great mystery in which the same Lord feeds his Church. So the witnesses make the verse the heart of the miracle's enactment: Christ takes the little store, gives thanks, breaks, and gives — and in the breaking and the giving the bread is multiplied, the imperfect tense (as Robertson presses) showing the wonder as an ongoing flow rather than a single instant. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold: the thankfulness with which Christ receives even the smallest provision from the Father's hand, and the ministry of the disciples, through whom the blessed bread reaches the people — so that the feeding of the four thousand becomes a figure of all Christ's provision, blessed by him, multiplied in the giving, and dispensed through the hands of those he sends.
7:
"And they had a few small fishes: and he blessed, and commanded to set them also before them." The witnesses note the small detail of the fishes and Mark's distinctive ordering. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "Mark mentions them last as if they were served after the food," differing slightly from Matthew's account; and Clarke notes the small fishes are "not noticed in the parallel place, Matthew 15:36," a touch peculiar to Mark. Gill records simply that the disciples "acquainted Christ with" the fishes "and brought them out unto him."

The expositors mark the second blessing: "he blessed, and commanded to set them also before them." As with the loaves, Christ first gives thanks, hallowing the provision before it is distributed; and the same multiplying power that filled the seven loaves fills the few fishes, so that both are made sufficient for the multitude.

So the witnesses make the verse a further instance of the method of the miracle. The fishes, like the loaves, are few — "a few small fishes," all the disciples had — yet Christ blesses them and they too are multiplied. The lesson the expositors draw is the same as on the loaves: that the Lord takes the whole slender store, blesses it, and makes it overflow; nothing the disciples bring is too small for his blessing, and the few fishes, surrendered and hallowed, feed thousands as truly as the bread. Mark's small detail, that the fishes were "blessed" with a distinct word and "set before them" after the loaves, the witnesses note, preserves the vividness of an eyewitness recollection, and underscores that every part of the provision passed first through Christ's thankful blessing before it reached the people — so that the whole feeding, loaves and fishes alike, is the work of the One who blesses the little and makes it abundant for the many. Mark's small detail, that the fishes were blessed with a distinct word and set before the people after the loaves, preserves the vividness of an eyewitness recollection and underscores that every part of the provision passed first through Christ's thankful blessing before it reached the multitude.
8:
"So they did eat, and were filled: and they took up of the broken meat that was left seven baskets." The witnesses dwell on the fulness of the satisfaction and the abundance of the remains. The Reformed Gill draws out the wonder: "they not only had some, but they had all enough, a full meal; it was surprising that it could be divided so, that every one should have a bit; but that they should all be satisfied to the full, is amazing." The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects "broken meat" to "fragments," and Robertson glosses the word for the remains as "overplus, abundance, remains of broken pieces not used, not just scraps or crumbs" — and notes the seven baskets (a different word, sphyridōn, from the twelve kophinoi of the first feeding).

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the doctrinal lesson: "this miracle was intended to demonstrate that Christ was the true bread which cometh down from heaven; for he who was almighty to create bread without means to support natural life, could not want power to create bread without means to support spiritual life. And this heavenly bread we stand so much in need of every moment, that we ought to be always praying, Lord, evermore give us this bread."

So the witnesses make the verse the proof and seal of the miracle. The multitude were not merely relieved but "filled," satisfied to the full; and the remains, far exceeding the original store, were gathered into seven baskets — the seven loaves multiplied into more than was begun with. The lesson the expositors press is twofold: the completeness of Christ's provision, which does not stint but satisfies to the full and leaves abundance over; and, with Wesley, the sign it gives of Christ himself as the bread of life, who feeds the body that he may point to the deeper hunger and the deeper supply. The seven baskets of fragments, the witnesses note, are both a memorial of the miracle and a pledge: that the Lord who multiplies bread for the hungry crowd is the same who gives himself, the true bread from heaven, in abundance that no need can exhaust.
9:
"And they that had eaten were about four thousand: and he sent them away." The witnesses note the number fed and the quiet dismissal, parallel to the first feeding. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill record that the four thousand were "men, besides women and children" (as Matthew specifies), so that the true number fed was far greater. The figure marks the scale of the miracle: a great multitude, satisfied to the full from seven loaves and a few fishes.

The dismissal, "he sent them away," answers the compassion with which the passage opened: Christ would not let them faint by the way, and now, having filled them, he sends them home in safety. Theophylact (on v.1) had drawn the lesson that "Christ sends no one away fasting, for He wishes all to be nourished by His grace" — the sending away here being not a turning off but the satisfied departure of those whom he has fed.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the miracle and the fulfilment of its mercy. The four thousand who had been three days with Christ, in danger of fainting on the long road home, are now fed and dismissed in peace. The lesson the expositors press, across this passage, is the completeness of Christ's care: he does not leave his work half done, relieving the hunger but neglecting the journey; having satisfied them, he sends them safely away. The number — four thousand men, with women and children beyond count — is recorded, the witnesses note, both as a testimony to the greatness of the miracle and as a measure of the compassion that wrought it, which would not send so great a multitude away unfed. The feeding of the four thousand thus stands, in the witnesses' reading, as a second seal upon the truth the disciples were so slow to learn: that the power and the pity of Christ are sufficient for every need, and that none who follow him will be sent away to faint, but all shall be nourished by his grace. The number — four thousand men, with women and children beyond count — is recorded both as a testimony to the greatness of the miracle and as a measure of the compassion that wrought it, a second seal upon the truth the disciples were so slow to learn: that the power and pity of Christ suffice for every need.
10:
"And straightway he entered into a ship with his disciples, and came into the parts of Dalmanutha." The witnesses note the swift departure and address a small harmonising question, then turn to the demand for a sign. The Latin Augustine reconciles Mark's "Dalmanutha" with Matthew's "Magdala": "we cannot doubt that it is the same place under another name, for several manuscripts even of St. Mark have only Magdala." Theophylact reads the departure as of a piece with the first feeding: Christ "retires, lest on account of the miracle the multitudes should take him to make him a king."

The fathers expound the sign sought from heaven. Theophylact: the Pharisees "wish him to make the sun and moon stand still, to bring down hail, and change the atmosphere; for they thought he could not perform miracles from heaven." The Lord refused, he says, because "the time of signs from heaven had not arrived, that is, the time of the second Advent, when the powers of heaven shall be shaken; but in the first Advent, all things are full of mercy." Bede adds that they wished him, "after the example of Moses, to refresh the whole nation with manna from heaven." Theophylact notes that Christ "quits the Pharisees as men uncorrected: where there is hope of correction, it is right to remain; but where the evil is incorrigible, we should go away."

So the witnesses make the verse the threshold of a new conflict. Hardly has Christ fed the four thousand by a miracle of mercy than the Pharisees meet him demanding a sign from heaven — the very thing his earthly works should have rendered needless. The lesson the expositors draw, here and across this section, is the obstinacy of unbelief, which is not satisfied by the miracles given but demands miracles of its own choosing; and the wisdom of Christ, who withholds the sign they ask, both because its time was not yet and because their hearts were hardened beyond persuasion. The swift crossing to Dalmanutha, and the swift departure that follows the refusal, mark, in the witnesses' reading, the Lord's withdrawal from those whose unbelief was incorrigible — leaving the Pharisees to their hardness and turning to instruct the disciples whose dulness was still curable.
11:
"And the Pharisees came forth, and began to question with him, seeking of him a sign from heaven, tempting him." The witnesses read the Pharisees' demand as a hostile snare, not an honest inquiry. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the verb "began to question" means "dispute, not mere inquiry," and that "they began at once and kept it up"; Matthew adds the Sadducees, "the first time these two parties appear together against Jesus" — though "the Pharisees and Herodians had already joined hands against Jesus in the sabbath controversy" (Mark 3:6). Wesley glosses "tempting him" as "trying to ensnare him."

The Puritan Henry draws the lesson from their obstinacy: "obstinate unbelief will have something to say, though ever so unreasonable. Christ refused to answer their demand. If they will not be convinced, they shall not." He turns it to lament: "what cause we have to lament for those around us, who destroy themselves and others by their perverse and obstinate unbelief, and enmity to the gospel!"

So the witnesses make the verse a portrait of unbelief in its most hardened form. The Pharisees come not to learn but to ensnare, demanding a sign from heaven from the very One whose works on earth had already proved his power. Their question is a dispute, kept up with settled malice; and behind it lies the obstinacy Henry names, which "will have something to say, though ever so unreasonable." The lesson the expositors press is the futility of fresh evidence for those resolved not to believe: Christ, who freely gave signs to the seeking, withholds them from the tempting, "for if they will not be convinced, they shall not." And Henry turns the warning inward, bidding us "chide ourselves severely" when we, like the disciples in the next verses, "forget the works of God and distrust him" — lest the unbelief we lament in the Pharisees be found, in lesser measure, in ourselves. Henry turns the warning inward, bidding us chide ourselves severely when we, like the disciples in the verses that follow, forget the works of God and distrust him, lest the unbelief we lament in the Pharisees be found, in lesser measure, in our own hearts.
12:
"And he sighed deeply in his spirit, and saith, Why doth this generation seek after a sign? verily I say unto you, There shall no sign be given unto this generation." The witnesses dwell on the deep sigh and the refusal of the sign. The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the sigh: Robertson notes the rare compound anastenaxas, the preposition intensifying it — "the sigh seemed to come from the bottom of his heart, the Lord's human spirit stirred to its depths" (Swete) — Jesus "resented the settled prejudice of the Pharisees." Gill reads it "on account of the hardness of their hearts, the malignity of their minds, and insincerity of their intentions, who had no view to come at truth, but to ensnare him." Clarke: "he was exceedingly affected at their obstinacy and hardness of heart."

On the refusal, Barnes and Ellicott note that Mark omits the exception Matthew records — "the sign of the prophet Jonas" — giving only the flat "no sign." Robertson observes Mark's Hebraic conditional form ("if a sign shall be given," a strong negation), and Barnes explains: "he said a sign should be given, the same as was furnished by Jonas; but this was not what they asked, nor would it be given because they asked it." Lightfoot supplies a rabbinic tale of signs demanded and refused, illustrating the temper Christ rebukes.

So the witnesses make the verse a revelation of the Saviour's grief over unbelief. The deep sigh, drawn from the depths of his human spirit, is the response not of anger only but of sorrow at a hardness that no evidence would move; and the refusal of the sign is its just answer — to those who demand miracles of their own choosing, not to be convinced but to ensnare, no sign shall be given. The lesson the expositors press is that Christ withholds further proof from the wilfully blind: the same Lord who sighs over their hardness will not gratify it, for a sign given to those resolved not to believe would only deepen their guilt. The only sign reserved for that generation, the witnesses note (with Matthew), was the sign of Jonah — his death and resurrection — the very proof they would at last reject, confirming that the fault lay never in want of evidence but in the obstinacy of a heart that would not see.
13:
"And he left them, and entering into the ship again departed to the other side." The witnesses read the brief verse as Christ's deliberate withdrawal from incorrigible unbelief. Gill states it plainly: he left them "as a perverse and hardened generation of men, and as such with whom it was not worth while to discourse." The leaving is judicial — a turning away from those whose hearts no sign would soften.

The expositors connect it with Theophylact's principle on the foregoing verses: "where there is hope of correction, it is right to remain; but where the evil is incorrigible, we should go away." Christ, having sighed over the Pharisees' hardness and refused their sign, now leaves them altogether, embarking again and departing to the other side. The withdrawal is at once a judgment on their unbelief and a deliverance of himself from a fruitless dispute, that he might turn to the disciples whose dulness was still curable.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the conflict at Dalmanutha and a solemn enactment of the Lord's dealing with hardened hearts. To demand a sign in order to ensnare, and to refuse the signs already given, is to forfeit Christ's presence; and so he "left them." The lesson the expositors draw is twofold: the danger of an obstinacy that drives Christ away, leaving the soul to its chosen blindness; and the example for his servants, who are not to cast their pearls before those who only trample them, but, where the evil is incorrigible, to withdraw. Yet the same departure that judges the Pharisees opens the way for the instruction of the disciples in the boat; for Christ leaves the hardened only to teach the teachable, and the withdrawal from unbelief at Dalmanutha becomes the occasion of the warning about the leaven and the patient questioning that follows. The Lord turns from those who would not learn to those who, however slowly, still could. Yet the same departure that judges the Pharisees opens the way for the instruction of the disciples in the boat; for Christ leaves the hardened only to teach the teachable, turning from those who would not learn to those who, however slowly, still could.
14:
"Now the disciples had forgotten to take bread, neither had they in the ship with them more than one loaf." The witnesses note the disciples' forgetfulness and a detail peculiar to Mark. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes "more than one loaf" is a "detail only in Mark — practically for thirteen men when hungry," and Ellicott corrects the connection: "and the disciples," in close link with the preceding verse, "not as the beginning of a new section." Gill notes they had "forgotten to take bread" at Dalmanutha "before they took shipping, as was their usual method."

The single loaf, the expositors note, sets the scene for the misunderstanding to come. Having just witnessed Christ feed four thousand from seven loaves, the disciples are anxious over having but one for themselves — the very anxiety the next verses will rebuke. Theophylact (on v.10) had read the forgetting as providential: "by a special providence the disciples forgot to take bread, that they might be blamed by Christ, and thus become better, and arrive at a knowledge of Christ's power."

So the witnesses make the verse the setup for the lesson on the leaven. The disciples' empty store and single loaf are the occasion of their carnal reasoning, which will mistake Christ's warning about the Pharisees' doctrine for a reproach about provisions. The lesson the expositors draw, with Theophylact, is that even their forgetfulness was overruled for good: it furnished the occasion by which Christ would expose and correct their dulness, and press upon them again the lesson of his power that the two feedings should have taught. The one loaf in the boat, the witnesses note, is a small but telling detail of Mark's eyewitness narrative; and it stands as a quiet irony, that men who had twice seen Christ multiply bread for thousands should be troubled over a single loaf among thirteen — an irony Christ himself will turn into a searching question in the verses that follow. The one loaf in the boat stands as a quiet irony, that men who had twice seen Christ multiply bread for thousands should be troubled over a single loaf among thirteen — an irony Christ himself will turn into a searching question, exposing and correcting their dulness in the verses that follow.
15:
"And he charged them, saying, Take heed, beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, and of the leaven of Herod." The witnesses expound the warning and note Mark's distinctive "leaven of Herod." The grammatical-historical commentators define the leaven as corrupt doctrine: Barnes explains that where Matthew has "the Sadducees," Mark has "Herod," and that Christ likely "cautioned them against them all" — the Pharisees who "sought his life," the Sadducees who "denied essential doctrines," and the Herodians who held "it lawful to acknowledge a foreign prince." Robertson sharpens it: Christ warns against "the leaven of Herod (bad politics) and the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees (bad theology and also bad politics)." Ellicott notes the imperfect tense, "he charged them," implying "the command was more than once repeated," so much was at stake.

Theophylact (on v.10) reads the leaven as "their hurtful and corrupt doctrine, full of the old malice," the Herodians being "the teachers who said that Herod was the Christ." The Wesleyan Wesley names them "two opposite extremes" — the Pharisees' superstition and Herod's (or the Sadducees') worldly laxity.

So the witnesses make the verse a solemn caution against false teaching from every quarter. The leaven, the expositors agree, is doctrine — corrupt, spreading, secretly pervasive, as leaven works through the lump (1 Corinthians 5:6) — and Christ warns against it whether it comes in the Pharisaic form of hypocrisy and tradition, the Sadducean form of unbelief, or the Herodian form of worldly compromise and bad politics. The lesson the witnesses press, with Robertson, is that the disciples "came out of a Pharisaic atmosphere and had just met it again at Dalmanutha"; the danger was real and near, and the warning, repeatedly charged, was needed. The two leavens Mark names — of the Pharisees and of Herod — represent, in the witnesses' reading, the twin perils of false religion and false worldliness, the corruption of the truth and the accommodation of it to power; against both the disciple is to be on his guard, lest the pure word be soured by either. The two leavens Mark names — of the Pharisees and of Herod — represent, in the witnesses' reading, the twin perils of false religion and false worldliness, the corruption of the truth and its accommodation to power; against both the disciple is to be on his guard, lest the pure word be soured by either.
16:
"And they reasoned among themselves, saying, It is because we have no bread." The witnesses note the disciples' carnal misunderstanding of the warning. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes the verb "reasoned one with another" implies "discussion," and the imperfect tense shows "they kept it up" — Matthew's "among themselves" being Mark's "with one another." Gill connects it to the foregoing: "upon Christ's giving this caution, and recollecting that they had forgot to buy provisions, they reasoned."

The disciples, the expositors agree, take Christ's spiritual warning about the Pharisees' doctrine for a literal reproach about bread. Their single loaf in the boat fills their minds, so that when Christ speaks of leaven they think only of the leaven that raises loaves, and suppose he is chiding them for having brought no provisions. The misunderstanding, the witnesses note, is the very dulness the next verses will rebuke.

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the carnal mind's incapacity for spiritual truth. The disciples hear a warning fraught with eternal meaning — against the corrupting doctrine of false teachers — and reduce it to a question of dinner. Their reasoning, kept up among themselves, betrays how earthbound their thoughts still were, and how readily they mistook the things of the Spirit for the things of the body. The lesson the expositors draw is the slowness of even the nearest disciples to rise from the literal to the spiritual, and the patience of Christ, who, though he will rebuke their dulness, does not cast them off for it. The one loaf that troubled them becomes, in the witnesses' reading, the measure of their distance from their Master's mind; for while Christ thought of the soul's danger from false doctrine, they thought only of the body's want of bread — a contrast Christ will press home in the searching questions that follow, until they learn at last that he spoke not of loaves but of the teaching of the Pharisees and of Herod. The one loaf that troubled them becomes, in the witnesses' reading, the measure of their distance from their Master's mind; for while Christ thought of the soul's danger from false doctrine, they thought only of the body's want of bread — a contrast Christ will press home in the searching questions that follow.
17:
"And when Jesus knew it, he saith unto them, Why reason ye, because ye have no bread? perceive ye not yet, neither understand? have ye your heart yet hardened?" The witnesses dwell on Christ's knowledge of their thoughts and the sharpness of his rebuke. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that Mark here gives "six keen questions of Jesus" (where Matthew compresses to four), revealing "the disappointment of Jesus at the intellectual dulness of his pupils" — touching "the intellect, the heart in a hardened state, the eyes, the ears, the memory." Gill notes that Christ "knew it immediately, by his omniscience," as he knew the thoughts of the Pharisees. Ellicott marks that "have ye your heart yet hardened?" is "peculiar to St. Mark," the indignant astonishment "characteristically more vivid and emphatic" in his report.

The Wesleyan Wesley weighs the strong words carefully: Christ "affirms of all the apostles that their hearts were hardened, that having eyes they saw not, having ears they heard not" — "the very same expressions that occur in the thirteenth of Matthew" of the unbelieving crowd. "And yet it is certain they were not judicially hardened; therefore all these strong expressions do not necessarily import anything more than the present want of spiritual understanding." Robertson agrees, citing Bruce: "for the time the Twelve are way-side hearers, with hearts like a beaten path, into which the higher truths cannot sink."

So the witnesses make the verse a searching exposure of the disciples' slowness, tempered by the assurance that it was not final hardness but a present dulness. Christ, who reads their reasoning, presses upon them question after question — do they not perceive, understand, remember? — that they may feel how far their carnal thoughts had strayed from his meaning. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold: the patience of the Teacher, who rebukes that he may instruct, and the persistence of unbelief even in the believing, whose hearts, for a time, were "hardened" not by reprobation but by a want of spiritual apprehension. The sharp words, the witnesses note, are the words used elsewhere of the obstinate crowd; yet here they are spoken not to condemn but to awaken, that the disciples, stung by the rebuke, might rise at last from the loaves to the leaven, from the body to the soul.
18:
"Having eyes, see ye not? and having ears, hear ye not? and do ye not remember?" The witnesses continue the chain of Christ's searching questions, drawn (as Robertson noted) from the language of Isaiah and applied here to the disciples' dulness. Gill reads the doubled sense: "meaning perhaps both the eyes of their bodies and of their understandings; they had bodily eyes, and with them saw the miracles he wrought, and yet took little notice; and the eyes of their understandings were enlightened by Christ, and yet saw things but very darkly."

The expositors note that these questions echo the words spoken of the unbelieving crowd (Isaiah 44:18; and Matthew 13 of those without), now turned with sorrowful surprise upon the disciples. They had eyes that had seen two feedings, ears that had heard Christ's teaching, and a memory that should have retained both; yet they reasoned as if they had seen, heard, and remembered nothing. The fault, the witnesses agree, is not in the faculties but in their use — eyes that saw without perceiving, ears that heard without understanding.

So the witnesses make the verse a probing of the disciples' failure to learn from what they had witnessed. The summons "do ye not remember?" leads directly to the appeal to the two feedings that follows; for the cure of their dulness lay in recollection. The lesson the expositors draw is that spiritual sight, hearing, and memory are more than the natural faculties: a man may see the works of Christ with his eyes and yet not perceive their meaning, hear his words with his ears and yet not understand, retain the facts in memory and yet draw no faith from them. The disciples' want, the witnesses note, was precisely this — a present blindness of the understanding, an enlightened eye that yet "saw things but very darkly," which Christ now presses them to feel, that, remembering the loaves and the baskets, they might rise from the dimness of carnal thought to the clear apprehension of his power. The disciples' want was precisely this — a present blindness of the understanding, an enlightened eye that yet saw very darkly — which Christ now presses them to feel, that, remembering the loaves and the baskets, they might rise from the dimness of carnal thought to the clear apprehension of his power.
19:
"When I brake the five loaves among five thousand, how many baskets full of fragments took ye up? They say unto him, Twelve." The witnesses note Christ's appeal to the first feeding and the disciples' exact recollection of its detail. The grammatical-historical Barnes cross-references the feeding of the five thousand (Mark 6:38, 44), and Robertson (on v.17) had observed that "the disciples did recall the number of baskets left over in each instance, twelve and seven," even distinguishing the two Greek words for the baskets — the kophinoi of the first feeding, the sphyrides of the second.

The point Christ presses, the expositors agree, is that the disciples remembered the facts perfectly yet drew no faith from them. They could name the number of baskets to the very figure, twelve; but the memory had not become trust. Their accurate recollection only deepens the rebuke: they had the evidence by heart and still reasoned as men with no bread.

So the witnesses make the verse part of Christ's method of curing dulness by recollection. He does not give the disciples new proof but turns them back to what they already knew, drawing from their own mouths the figure that should have silenced their anxiety. The lesson the expositors draw is the difference between remembering a mercy and trusting the God of it: the disciples could rehearse the miracle exactly, yet had not learned its lesson, that the One who fed five thousand from five loaves with twelve baskets to spare could surely provide for thirteen men with one. The twelve baskets, named so precisely, become in the witnesses' reading both a memorial of Christ's power and a measure of the disciples' slowness — for the very exactness of their memory shows that their failure was not of fact but of faith, a heart that held the evidence yet would not draw the conclusion. Christ presses the recollection home that the conclusion, so long resisted, might at last be reached. The twelve baskets, named so precisely, become both a memorial of Christ's power and a measure of the disciples' slowness; for the very exactness of their memory shows that their failure was not of fact but of faith, a heart that held the evidence yet would not draw the conclusion.
20:
"And when the seven among four thousand, how many baskets full of fragments took ye up? And they said, Seven." The witnesses note the second appeal, to the feeding just past, and the disciples' precise answer. The grammatical-historical Barnes cross-references the feeding of the four thousand earlier in this chapter (Mark 8:1-9), and the witnesses observe that the disciples again recall the exact number — seven baskets — distinguishing it from the twelve of the first feeding.

Christ's double appeal, the expositors agree, presses the lesson by repetition. He has fed two great multitudes from a few loaves, with abundance over each time; the disciples remember both, and the very figures — twelve and seven — they can name without hesitation. Yet from these two unanswerable proofs of his power to provide, they had drawn no confidence for their present want of bread.

So the witnesses make the verse the completion of Christ's argument from memory. Two feedings, two sets of baskets, two exact recollections — and still the disciples had reasoned as men with no bread. The lesson the expositors draw is the weight of accumulated evidence against persistent unbelief: not one miracle but two, not vaguely but precisely remembered, lay in the disciples' own knowledge, and yet their faith had not risen to meet their need. The seven baskets of the second feeding, set beside the twelve of the first, become in the witnesses' reading a double witness that the Lord who twice multiplied bread for thousands could not lack power for thirteen — a witness the disciples carried in their memory yet failed to read. Christ rehearses both, that the cumulative force of what they themselves remembered might break through their dulness and bring them, in the next verse, to the question that gathers up the whole: "How is it that ye do not understand?" The seven baskets of the second feeding, set beside the twelve of the first, become a double witness that the Lord who twice multiplied bread for thousands could not lack power for thirteen — a witness the disciples carried in their memory yet failed to read, until Christ pressed both upon them together.
21:
"And he said unto them, How is it that ye do not understand?" The witnesses read this closing question as the climax of Christ's rebuke and the turning-point toward understanding. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws out its force: "after all this rebuke and explanation. The greatest of all teachers had the greatest of all classes, but he struck a snag here." Yet he notes the outcome Matthew records: "Then they understood how that he bade them not beware of the loaves of bread, but of the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees" — the lesson, pressed home, was at last learned. "The teacher must have patience if his pupils are to understand." Gill connects it to the foregoing: "since this was the case, and they so well remembered the miracles, and the circumstances of them," how is it they still did not perceive?

The question, the expositors agree, is not despairing but instructive. Christ has marshalled the whole evidence — two feedings, two sets of baskets, exactly remembered — and now asks how, with all this in their memory, they still fail to grasp that he spoke of doctrine, not of bread. The wonder of the question is the measure of the proof they had resisted.

So the witnesses make the verse the seal of Christ's patient labour to cure dulness. The repeated questions, the appeal to the feedings, the precise recollections — all converge on this final "How is it that ye do not understand?" The lesson the expositors draw is twofold: the depth of the slowness even of the chosen disciples, who held the evidence yet missed the meaning; and the patience of the Teacher, who did not abandon them to their dulness but pressed the lesson until, as Matthew tells, "then they understood." The rebuke, the witnesses note, was severe but effectual; it shamed them out of their carnal reasoning and opened their minds to the spiritual warning Christ had given, that the leaven to fear was not the leaven of bread but the corrupting doctrine of the Pharisees and of Herod. Christ's question, then, is at once the sharpest word of the rebuke and the door through which the disciples passed from misunderstanding to understanding.
22:
"And he cometh to Bethsaida; and they bring a blind man unto him, and besought him to touch him." The witnesses introduce the unique two-stage healing and read both its setting and its symbolism. The Latin Bede notes that those who brought the man knew "the touch of the Lord could give sight as well as cleanse a leper," and that Christ "took the blind man by the hand and led him out of the town." He draws the spiritual sense: "the Lord touches us when He enlightens our minds with the breath of His Spirit, and stirs us up that we may recognise our own infirmity and be diligent in good actions." And he reads the gradual cure as deliberate: "though by one word He could cure the man wholly and all at once, still He cures him by degrees, to shew the greatness of the blindness of man, which can hardly, step by step, be restored to light."

Theophylact reads Bethsaida's unbelief as the reason for both the leading-out and the gradualness: "Bethsaida appears to have been infected with much infidelity, wherefore the Lord reproaches it (Matthew 11:21); He takes the blind man out of the town, for the faith of those who brought him was not true faith." And "the reason he did not see at once perfectly, but in part, was that he had not perfect faith; for healing is bestowed in proportion to faith."

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the only recorded miracle Christ wrought by stages, and they find in that singularity its meaning. Bede and Theophylact agree the gradual cure was no failure of power but a lesson: of the depth of human blindness, restored only "step by step"; of the imperfect faith of Bethsaida, which received a healing in proportion; and (as the later verses will draw out) of the spiritual illumination of the soul, which often dawns dimly before it sees clearly. The leading of the blind man by the hand out of the unbelieving town is itself, in the witnesses' reading, a parable of grace — Christ taking the helpless, drawing him apart from the influence of unbelief, and dealing with him alone, that the light might come where the faith to receive it could grow.
23:
"And he took the blind man by the hand, and led him out of the town; and when he had spit on his eyes, and put his hands upon him, he asked him if he saw ought." The witnesses dwell on the leading-out, the spittle, and the question. The grammatical-historical Barnes weighs the reasons for leading him from the town: perhaps "to avoid the collecting of a multitude, and the designs of the Pharisees," choosing "to perform the miracle alone; thus showing that while he did good he desired to do it in such a way as to avoid the appearance of evil, and to prevent ostentation." Clarke reads it more sternly, that Christ "considered them unworthy of another miracle wrought among them," having "deeply deplored their ingratitude and obstinacy" (Matthew 11:21).

On the spittle, the witnesses agree it was not a natural remedy but a sign. Barnes: "it was to the man a sign, an evidence, that it was the power of Jesus"; the eyes were "probably gummed, united together," and "to wet them would be a sign, a natural expression of removing the obstruction" — "the power was not in the spittle, but it attended the application of it." Clarke adds that the spittle might merely "separate the eyelids," which "in certain cases of blindness are found always gummed together," while "it required a miracle to restore the sight."

So the witnesses make the verse the enactment of the first stage of the cure, and they find in its details both the prudence and the method of Christ. He leads the man apart, away from the crowd and the hostile Pharisees, that the miracle might be done without ostentation or danger; he uses the spittle and the touch not as natural means of healing but as visible signs, accommodated to the blind man's understanding, that he might know the power that healed him; and he asks "if he saw ought," not from ignorance, but (as the next verses show) for the disciples' sake and the man's, to mark the dawning of sight. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ's miracles vindicate themselves "by their obvious utility," never working by natural means what nature could do, but joining sign to power, that the recipient might both receive the gift and know its Giver.
24:
"And he looked up, and said, I see men as trees, walking." The witnesses fasten on this vivid description of half-restored sight and its symbolic depth. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the naturalness of the words: Robertson calls it "a vivid description of dawning sight — his vision was incomplete though he could tell that they were men because they were walking; this is the single case of a gradual cure in the healings wrought by Jesus." Ellicott renders the two verbs precisely — "I behold men, for I see them walking as trees" — "his sight was not yet clear, but he interpreted what it told him rightly," and adds that "the naturalness of this description of the first impression of the restored sense strikes every reader."

The Reformed Calvin explains the purpose: "our Lord had put the question for the sake of his disciples, to inform them that the man had received something, but that hitherto nothing more than a slight commencement of the cure had been effected." The man "speaks only by conjecture when he says that they are men," distinguishing them from trees only by their motion.

Ellicott draws out the symbolic reading that the fathers and moderns alike find here: "from the point of view which looks on our Lord's miracles as acted parables, we may see in it that which represents an analogous stage in the spiritual growth of men, when truths for which before they had no faculty of vision are seen for the first time, but are not yet apprehended in their full proportions. They need a second touch of the Divine Hand, the passing away of another film of ignorance or prejudice, and then they too see all things clearly." So the witnesses make the verse the heart of the chapter's most distinctive sign. The half-cured man, seeing men only as moving trees, is at once a true picture of dawning natural sight — vivid, conjectural, incomplete — and a parable of the soul's gradual illumination, which often perceives spiritual truth dimly before it sees it whole. The lesson the expositors draw is that grace, like this cure, may come by degrees: the first touch gives sight, but not yet clear sight; and the soul newly enlightened, like the disciples themselves in this chapter, sees enough to know it sees, yet needs a further touch before all things stand plain.
25:
"After that he put his hands again upon his eyes, and made him look up: and he was restored, and saw every man clearly." The witnesses dwell on the second touch and the completed cure. The grammatical-historical Gill notes the detail of method: "from hence it seems plain that he first spit on his eyes, and then closed them, and put his hands on them; which last action he repeated, though not the former." Clarke records a textual variant — that the man "saw all things" (hapanta) rather than "all men" — "for the view he had of them before was indistinct and confused."

Clarke draws out the sovereignty in the method: "our Lord could have restored this man to sight in a moment; but he chose to do it in the way mentioned, to show that he is sovereign of his own graces; and to point out that, however small the first manifestations of mercy may be, they are nevertheless the beginnings of the fullness of the blessings of the Gospel of peace." Barnes develops the spiritual analogy at length: sinners are by nature blind (2 Corinthians 4:4); religion opens the eyes, yet "at first he sees indistinctly," and "it is only by repeated application to the Source of Light that he sees all things clearly."

So the witnesses make the verse the completion of the unique two-stage miracle and the seal of its lesson. The second laying-on of hands brings the dim sight to full clearness: the man, who had seen men as walking trees, now "saw every man clearly." The lesson the expositors draw, both of the body and the soul, is that Christ is "sovereign of his own graces," giving sight as he wills, sometimes by degrees; and that the dawning of spiritual light, often dim and confused at first, is brought to fulness only by the repeated touch of the same Hand. Barnes turns it to comfort for the perplexed believer: "let him not despair; light, in due time, will be shed on these obscure truths," and "faithful and repeated application to the Father of Lights" will bring the soul, like this man, from seeing men as trees to seeing all things clearly. The second touch, the witnesses note, is as necessary as the first, and the patience of Christ in completing what he begins is the pledge that the work of grace, however gradual, will not be left half-done.
26:
"And he sent him away to his house, saying, Neither go into the town, nor tell it to any in the town." The witnesses note the dismissal and the charge of silence, reading both prudence and spiritual discipline. The grammatical-historical commentators connect the prohibition to Bethsaida's unbelief: Robertson notes "he was not allowed to enter the village and create excitement before Jesus moved on to Caesarea Philippi"; Barnes, that it was "lest it excite the jealousy of the Pharisees, and produce commotion and danger." Clarke infers the man "did not belong to Bethsaida, for in going to his house he was not to enter the village."

The Reformed Calvin reads it of Christ's purpose for his miracles: "no miracle was performed by him in order to remain perpetually buried, but he intended to have it concealed, along with many others, till, after having expiated by his death the sins of the world, he should ascend to the glory of the Father." Ellicott draws a spiritual discipline from the charge: "our Lord seems to have prescribed quietude after, as well as before, the miracle — because the work that had been done called for prayer for the right use of the new power, and because He would not seem to court the fame of publicity."

So the witnesses make the verse the quiet close of the Bethsaida miracle and a window into Christ's reserve. He sends the healed man home, charging him not to publish the cure in the unbelieving town nor to enter it; and the witnesses find in this both a judgment on Bethsaida, deprived of the fame and the further light it had despised, and a discipline for the man, bidden to retire and not to noise abroad what God had done. The lesson the expositors draw, following the symbolic reading of the gradual cure, is that "the first clear apprehension of spiritual truths should not be followed by the hasty utterances of the excitement of the new-born life" (Ellicott): the newly enlightened, like the newly healed, are to seek quietude and prayer for the right use of their sight, rather than rush to display it. Christ's charge of silence, the witnesses note, guards both the man and his miracle — withholding it from the hardened town, and preserving it, with so many others, for the day when, his death accomplished and his glory revealed, the whole work of the Saviour should be proclaimed.
27:
"And Jesus went out, and his disciples, into the towns of Caesarea Philippi: and by the way he asked his disciples, saying unto them, Whom do men say that I am?" The witnesses read this as the great turning-point, where Christ draws out the confession of his Person before unveiling the cross. Theophylact notes that "after taking his disciples afar from the Jews, he then asks them concerning himself, that they might speak the truth without fear of the Jews." Bede explains the place — Philip's city, named "in honour of Tiberius Caesar" — and the method: "he first asks what is the opinion of men, in order to try the faith of the disciples, lest their confession should appear founded on the common opinion."

The fathers note the popular guesses and the deeper purpose. The opinions — John the Baptist, Elijah, one of the prophets — show that "many thought John had risen from the dead, as even Herod believed" (Theophylact). But Christ asks the common view, Bede says, only to set the disciples' confession apart from it; and Pseudo-Chrysostom: "He asks the question with a purpose, for it was right that his disciples should praise him better than" the crowd.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the chapter's climax. Christ leads his disciples north, away from the hostile Jews and the unbelieving towns, to the borders of Caesarea Philippi, and there, "by the way," begins the great catechism of his Person. The lesson the expositors draw is the method of the Teacher: he first asks what men say, that the disciples, hearing the inadequacy of the popular guesses, might be led to a truer confession of their own. The question "Whom do men say that I am?" is, in the witnesses' reading, the preface to the deeper question that follows; for Christ would have his disciples own him not on the report of the crowd but on the conviction of their own hearts, that, the confession once made, he might begin to teach them the mystery of the cross which the crowd could not bear. The withdrawal to a place of quiet and safety, the witnesses note, frees the disciples to speak the truth without fear; and so the scene is set for the confession that is the hinge of the Gospel.
28:
"And they answered, John the Baptist: but some say, Elias; and others, One of the prophets." The witnesses note the popular opinions the disciples report, all honourable yet all wide of the truth. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes "they knew only too well," and refers to the fuller discussion at Matthew 16:14. Barnes cross-references Herod's own theory that Jesus was "John the Baptist" risen (Matthew 14:2).

The opinions, the expositors agree, share a common root: all suppose Jesus to be some great figure of the past returned to life — John, Elijah, or one of the prophets — rather than the Messiah himself. The people, esteeming antiquity above the present, could not allow that so extraordinary a Person could be one of their own age, and so made him any of the ancients rather than own him as the Christ. The guesses are reverent, even high; but they fall short, because they make him a forerunner or a prophet, not the One the prophets foretold.

So the witnesses make the verse the foil against which Peter's confession will shine. The disciples rehearse the various and honourable but mistaken verdicts of the crowd, each of which exalts Jesus yet none of which reaches the truth. The lesson the expositors draw, here as in the parallel at Matthew, is that it is possible to think highly of Christ and still not highly enough — to take him for a prophet, a forerunner, a risen saint, and yet miss that he is the Son of the living God. The popular opinions, the witnesses note, were the best the unaided mind could reach; and their very inadequacy prepares for the confession that follows, which no flesh and blood could reveal, but only the Father. By setting the crowd's guesses first, Christ leads the disciples to feel the distance between common opinion and saving faith, that the truth they are about to confess might be seen for what it is — a gift of revelation, not a conclusion of report. The popular opinions were the best the unaided mind could reach, and their very inadequacy prepares for the confession that follows, which no flesh and blood could reveal, but only the Father — so that by setting the crowd's guesses first, Christ leads the disciples to feel the distance between common opinion and saving faith.
29:
"And he saith unto them, But whom say ye that I am? And Peter answereth and saith unto him, Thou art the Christ." The witnesses read this as the heart of the chapter, the confession on which all turns, and they note Mark's distinctive brevity. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "Mark does not give 'the Son of the living God' (Matthew 16:16) or 'of God' (Luke 9:20)"; "the full confession is the form in Matthew," yet "the form in Mark really means the full idea." Gill notes the question's purpose: "it was for the sake of this question he put the former." Clarke records that three manuscripts add "the Son of the living God."

Robertson presses the weight of the moment: the disciples "had confessed him as Messiah before" (John 1:41; 4:29; 6:69; Matthew 14:33), "but Jesus had ceased to use the word Messiah to avoid political complications"; the question is "a serious test" — "did the disciples still believe in Jesus as Messiah after all the defections and oppositions seen by them?" Peter's answer, brief and bold, is the answer of faith holding firm where the crowd had fallen away.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax toward which the whole scene has moved. Against the inadequate guesses of the crowd, Peter, speaking for the Twelve, confesses Jesus to be "the Christ" — the Messiah promised to the fathers, not a forerunner or a risen prophet but the Anointed himself. The lesson the expositors draw is that this confession, brief in Mark's report yet full in its meaning, is the rock of true faith: it marks the disciples off from the multitude, not by a higher opinion but by a divine conviction. Mark's terseness, the witnesses note (with Robertson and Ellicott on the next verse), may reflect Peter's own humility in the telling, omitting the praise Matthew records; but the substance is the same great confession. By drawing it from his disciples before he speaks of the cross, Christ secures the foundation on which alone the hard teaching of his suffering can be received — that they who own him Messiah may learn what manner of Messiah he came to be.
30:
"And he charged them that they should tell no man of him." The witnesses note the charge of silence after the confession, and they read its reasons. The grammatical-historical Robertson explains "of him" as "being the Messiah, that he was the Christ (Matthew 16:20); not yet, for the time was not yet ripe; when that comes, the triumphal entry, the very stones will cry out if men will not (Luke 19:40)." The Wesleyan Wesley gives three reasons: "that he might not encourage the people to set him up for a temporal king; that he might not provoke the scribes and Pharisees to destroy him before the time; and that he might not forestall the bright evidence to be given of his Divine character after his resurrection."

Ellicott draws a notable inference from Mark's omission: where Matthew records Christ's full praise of Peter, Mark — on the assumption of his connection with Peter — leaves it out, "fairly regarded as an evidence of the humility of the Apostle, who shrank from what might seem to savour of self-assertion." Gill notes that Mark passes over the promise of the keys and the rock, which Matthew relates, and gives only "the strict and severe charge."

So the witnesses make the verse a lesson in the timing of testimony. The great confession, once made, was not to be published — not because it was false, but because its hour had not come. The lesson the expositors draw is the wisdom of Christ in withholding the open proclamation of his Messiahship: to declare it then would have inflamed the people's hope of an earthly king, hastened the malice of his enemies, and forestalled the decisive proof of his resurrection. The disciples were to hold the truth, but to keep it, until the cross and the empty tomb should set it in its true light. Mark's silence about the praise of Peter, the witnesses note, is itself eloquent — the mark, on the traditional view, of Peter's own humility in the telling; so that the very Gospel which records the confession most briefly, and omits its reward, bears witness to the lowliness of the man who made it, and to the Lord's care that the confession be guarded till its time.
31:
"And he began to teach them, that the Son of man must suffer many things, and be rejected of the elders, and of the chief priests, and scribes, and be killed, and after three days rise again." The witnesses mark this as the new stage of teaching that the confession made possible. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "Mark is fond of this idiom" ("began to teach"), but here it is no mere device: Matthew says "from that time," for "they had to be told soon about the approaching death of Jesus; the confession of faith indicated that it was a good time to begin." He details the agents — "death at the hands of the Sanhedrin (elders, chief priests, scribes)" — and the resurrection "on the third day, mentioned, but it made no impression on their minds; this rainbow on the cloud was not seen." On Mark's "after three days," Robertson insists it has "the same sense as Matthew and Luke's 'the third day,' else they are hopelessly contradictory."

The witnesses stress the necessity — "the Son of man must suffer." Cyril of Jerusalem draws out that Christ "came to his passion willingly": "others, who foreknow it not, die unwillingly; but he spoke before of his passion... It was lest the whole world should perish in its sins." Gill notes Christ now told them plainly "what as yet he had not said in express terms," because, "being firmly established in the faith of him as the Messiah," they could bear it.

So the witnesses make the verse the unveiling of the cross, set deliberately after the confession of the Person. Having drawn from his disciples the avowal that he is the Christ, Christ "begins to teach" them what manner of Christ he came to be — not a triumphant earthly king but a suffering, rejected, and slain Redeemer, who must die and rise. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine necessity of the passion: the "must" that governs the whole — he must suffer, be rejected, be killed, and rise — marks it not as misfortune but as the appointed work of redemption, foreknown and freely undertaken. The mention of the resurrection, the witnesses note, fell on deaf ears; the disciples, fixed on the dreadful word "be killed," could not yet see the "rainbow on the cloud." Yet Christ joins the rising to the dying from the first, that when the cross came it might not overwhelm their faith, having been foretold by the One who went to it willingly, that the world might not perish in its sins.
32:
"And he spake that saying openly. And Peter took him, and began to rebuke him." The witnesses fasten on the plainness of Christ's speech and the presumption of Peter's rebuke. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on "openly": Robertson glosses parrēsia as "he held back nothing, told it all, without reserve, to all of them," the imperfect tense showing "Jesus did it repeatedly" — and notes this item is "Mark alone." Gill draws out the boldness: Christ "spake it not only before them all, but in plain words, without a figure... very freely, and likewise boldly, with an undaunted courage, intrepidity of mind, being not in the least discouraged."

On Peter's rebuke, Wesley notes the vivid touch "Peter taking hold of him — perhaps by the arms or clothes," and Robertson (on the next verse) that "Peter had called Jesus off to himself," remonstrating "as a leader of the Twelve." The expositors mark the boldness on both sides: Christ speaking the hard truth of his death without reserve, and Peter, scandalised by it, daring to take his Master aside and chide him.

So the witnesses make the verse the meeting of Christ's plain foretelling with Peter's carnal recoil. The Lord, having confessed himself Messiah's hard road, now speaks it "openly," with an undaunted courage that hides nothing of the shame and suffering to come; and Peter, unable to reconcile so dark a destiny with the glorious Christ he had just confessed, takes him aside to rebuke him. The lesson the expositors draw is the collision of two minds: Christ's, set with resolute clearness on the cross as the way of redemption; and Peter's, which loved his Master but "savoured the things of men," recoiling from the very suffering that was the world's salvation. The plainness of Christ's speech, the witnesses note, left no room for misunderstanding — it was Peter's affection, not his comprehension, that failed; and his presumption in rebuking the One he had owned as Christ sets the stage for the sternest rebuke Christ ever gave a disciple, which the next verse records. The plainness of Christ's speech left no room for misunderstanding; it was Peter's affection, not his comprehension, that failed, and his presumption in rebuking the One he had just owned as Christ sets the stage for the sternest rebuke Christ ever gave a disciple, which the next verse records.
33:
"But when he had turned about and looked on his disciples, he rebuked Peter, saying, Get thee behind me, Satan: for thou savourest not the things that be of God, but the things that be of men." The witnesses dwell on the sternness of the rebuke and the detail, peculiar to Mark, that Christ first looked on the disciples. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws it out: Jesus "quickly wheeled round on Peter" (epistrapheis), and "in doing that the other disciples were in plain view also (this touch only in Mark); hence Jesus rebukes Peter in the full presence of the whole group" — for it is even possible "the others shared Peter's views." Wesley notes he looked on his disciples "that they might the more observe what he said to Peter."

Theophylact (on v.27) explains the name "Satan": Christ "called Peter Satan, because he savoured the things of Satan, and, from unwillingness that Christ should suffer, became his adversary; for Satan is interpreted 'the adversary.'" He notes Peter "in some way savoured carnal affections, for he wished that Christ should spare himself and not be crucified." Robertson presses the gravity: Peter "had not risen above the level of ordinary men and deserves the name of Satan whose role he was now acting; the temptation of the devil on the mountain was here offered by Peter — it was Satan over again."

So the witnesses make the verse the chapter's most startling moment: the disciple just honoured for his confession now called "Satan." The lesson the expositors draw is the peril of a love that opposes God's will. Peter, out of affection, sought to turn Christ from the cross; but in so doing he became, for the moment, the mouthpiece of the tempter, renewing the wilderness offer of a crown without a cross. His fault, the witnesses note, was not malice but a carnal mind — he "savoured the things of men," measuring Christ's mission by human notions of glory and safety, not by the divine necessity of redemption through suffering. Christ's rebuke, severe as it is, is also a deliverance: he turns and looks on all the disciples, that the lesson might reach them too, and orders Peter "behind" him — not to cast him off, but to restore him to the place of a follower, who walks after his Master to the cross rather than standing in his way.
34:
"And when he had called the people unto him with his disciples also, he said unto them, Whosoever will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me." The witnesses read this as the law of discipleship, addressed now to the crowd as well as the Twelve. The Antiochene Chrysostom stresses its freedom: "I call you to those good things which a man should wish for; I do not force you to evil and burdensome things, for he who does violence to his hearer often stands in his way, but he who leaves him free rather draws him to himself." He explains self-denial: "a man denies himself when he cares not for his body, so that whether it be scourged or whatever it suffer, he bears it patiently" — not merely not sparing himself, but denying himself "as if he had nothing in common with himself," facing danger "as if another were suffering." And the cross "even to the most shameful death"; yet one must suffer "for Christ's sake," conforming "to his death," else he does not follow Christ.

The Latin Bede defines the terms: "we deny ourselves when we avoid what we were of old, and strive to reach that point whither we are newly called; and the cross is taken up when either our body is pained by abstinence, or our soul afflicted by fellow-feeling for our neighbour." He notes the saying applies both "in time of persecution," when "our life is to be laid aside," and "in time of peace," when "our earthly desires are to be broken."

So the witnesses make the verse the great charter of following Christ, set deliberately after the prediction of his own cross. Having foretold his suffering and rebuked Peter for recoiling from it, Christ now calls all who would come after him to the same path — self-denial, cross-bearing, and following. The lesson the expositors draw is that discipleship is conformity to the crucified: not a forced burden but a free choice, offered to "whosoever will," yet costing nothing less than the renouncing of self and the taking up of a cross. To deny oneself, with Chrysostom, is to hold one's own body and life as another's, bearing all for Christ; to take up the cross is to accept suffering, even to death, for his sake; and to follow is to walk after him in the very way of his passion. The call addressed to the multitude, the witnesses note, makes plain that the cross is not for the Twelve alone but for all who would be Christ's — the universal and abiding condition of true discipleship.
35:
"For whosoever will save his life shall lose it; but whosoever shall lose his life for my sake and the gospel's, the same shall save it." The witnesses receive this paradox as the ground of the call to cross-bearing, and they note Mark's distinctive addition. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "and the gospel's sake" is "in Mark alone," and Ellicott marks its significance: where Matthew has simply "for my sake," Mark's addition shows that "though our Lord demanded entire personal devotion, it was for Himself as identified with the cause of the good news from God of which He was to be the martyr" (John 18:37). The two senses of "life" and "save," Robertson notes, turn the saying on a paradox.

Gill expounds the contrast: a man may "save his life for the present" by denying Christ and giving up the gospel, but he loses it eternally; while he who is willing to lay it down for Christ's sake "shall save it." The Wesleyan Wesley simply cross-references the many parallels (Matthew 16:25; Luke 17:33; John 12:25), the saying being among the most repeated of Christ's words.

So the witnesses make the verse the rationale of self-denial and the cross. To grasp at the present life by forsaking Christ is to forfeit the true and eternal life; to surrender the present life for Christ and his gospel is to gain it. The lesson the expositors press is the eternal arithmetic of discipleship: the cross is not loss but gain, for the life laid down "for my sake and the gospel's" is not destroyed but saved. Mark's addition "and the gospel's," the witnesses note, is no small thing: it binds devotion to Christ inseparably to the cause of the good news he came to proclaim and to die for, so that to suffer for the gospel is to suffer for Christ himself. The paradox, the witnesses agree, overturns the world's reckoning — the self-saving lose, the self-losing save — and grounds the whole call of the preceding verse, for it shows that the path of the cross, which seems the way of loss, is in truth the only way to keep the life that matters.
36:
"For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?" The witnesses read this as the great weighing of the soul against all the world, the ground of the call to lose life for Christ. Gill, who carries the comment, presses the futility of the worldly bargain: a man may, "by denying Christ and his Gospel," not only "save his life for the present, but procure for himself great riches and wealth" — yet "in the long run, in the issue of things," it profits him nothing, if he loses his soul.

The expositors, here and in the parallel at Matthew 16, make the verse the supreme statement of the soul's worth. The whole world, with all its riches and gain, is set in the scale against a single soul and found too light; for the world is temporal and the soul eternal, and to win the one at the cost of the other is the most ruinous of all exchanges. The "profit" the world offers is no profit at all, if the soul be lost in the gaining.

So the witnesses make the verse the decisive argument for the path of self-denial. The cross, which the world counts loss, is gain; the world's gain, bought by forsaking Christ and so losing the soul, is the deepest loss. The lesson the expositors draw is the incomparable value of the soul and the folly of the bargain that would trade it for the world. A man who, to save his present life or to gain its wealth, denies Christ, may seem to prosper; but he has bartered the eternal for the temporal, and at the issue of things stands stripped of all, having gained a world he cannot keep and lost a soul he cannot recover.
