The question Christ poses — "what shall it profit?" — admits, in the witnesses' reading, of only one answer: nothing; and that answer is the seal of all the preceding call, that the disciple who loses his life for Christ has made the only profitable exchange, and the worldling who saves it has made the most disastrous, for the soul outweighs the world, and to lose it is to lose all.
37:
"Or what shall a man give in exchange for his soul?" The witnesses read this as the sealing question of the foregoing, pressing home that the soul, once lost, is beyond all ransom. Gill, who carries the comment, draws it out: "to deliver it out of its miserable state and condition, all the riches of the world, and the whole world itself, are not an equivalent to it, or a sufficient ransom for it." Riches "will not profit in the day of wrath, or deliver a soul from damnation and ruin"; so that, "if he had the whole world, he could not redeem his soul with it; and he has nothing else to give for it, and therefore it is past all recovery."

The expositors, with the parallel at Matthew, make the verse the climax of the argument for the soul's worth. The previous verse weighed the soul against the world and found the world too light; this verse presses the consequence: there is no price a man can pay to buy back a soul once forfeited. The whole world, even if he had it, would not suffice; and he has nothing greater to offer. The loss is therefore irretrievable.

So the witnesses make the verse the most solemn warning of the chapter. Having shown that to gain the world and lose the soul is no profit, Christ now shows that the loss is final: no exchange, no ransom, no after-payment can recover a soul that is lost. The lesson the expositors draw is the unspeakable preciousness of the soul and the absolute folly of risking it. A man who barters his soul for the world cannot, when he sees his error, buy it back; the world he gained is no equivalent, and he has nothing else to give. The question Christ asks — "what shall a man give in exchange for his soul?" — is, in the witnesses' reading, unanswerable, and its very unanswerableness is the warning: since nothing can ransom a lost soul, the only wisdom is not to lose it, but to deny self, take up the cross, and follow Christ, who alone can save it. The verse thus seals the whole call of the passage, leaving the hearer with the choice between the soul and the world, and the knowledge that the choice, once wrongly made, can never be undone.
38:
"Whosoever therefore shall be ashamed of me and of my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, of him also shall the Son of man be ashamed, when he cometh in the glory of his Father with the holy angels." The witnesses read this as the solemn close of the discipleship discourse, joining present confession to future judgment. The grammatical-historical Barnes expounds the shame: to be "ashamed to own attachment to me on account of my lowly appearance, my poverty, contempt, and sufferings," and of "my doctrines, my instructions." The sentence is exact: "whosoever shall refuse, through pride or wickedness, to acknowledge and serve Christ here, shall be excluded from his kingdom hereafter" — "they that would not acknowledge him here must be rejected by him there." The contrast of the two comings is sharp: "he was lowly, meek, and despised... but he will come again in awful grandeur — not as the babe of Bethlehem, not as the Man of Nazareth, but as the Son of God, in majesty and glory."

The Wesleyan Clarke probes the cause of the offence: the Jews "saw nothing in the person of Jesus which corresponded to the pompous notions they had formed of the Messiah"; "a suffering Messiah must be acknowledged, and poverty and affliction must be borne" — "of him, and of his words, in this sense, the world is, to this day, ashamed." And he draws the terrible consequence: "as he refused to acknowledge me before men, so will I refuse to acknowledge him before God and his angels — and who can help him whom the only Saviour eternally disowns?"

So the witnesses make the verse the final seal of the call to take up the cross. To follow Christ is to confess him openly, even in his lowliness and the offence of his cross, before "this adulterous and sinful generation"; to be ashamed of him now is to be disowned by him at his coming in glory. The lesson the expositors press is the reciprocity of confession and reward: the shame a man refuses to bear for Christ here, Christ will visit upon him there; the Son of man who came in humility will return "in the glory of his Father with the holy angels," and will own before that glorious court only those who owned him before men. Bede (on v.34) had noted that this glimpse of "everlasting joy" was given "with a loving foresight, that they, having tasted for a brief moment the contemplation of everlasting joy, might with greater strength bear up under adversity." So the warning is also an encouragement: the cross borne now, and the confession made now in the face of a hostile generation, will be answered by acknowledgment in the day of glory — while the shame now indulged will be answered by an eternal disowning, from which there is no appeal.
